meta.author: Leana Le
meta.purpose: Portfolio case studies with prototype-aware content
meta.notes: All projects are student projects. No fabricated metrics. Prototypes appear after their referenced sections.

case_studies.title: ProLog
case_studies.subtitle: A journey companion for neurodivergent apprentices
case_studies.summary: A centralized apprenticeship companion helping neurodivergent apprentices track progress, reduce cognitive overload, and build confidence through clear visualization and structured tools.
case_studies.context: School project in collaboration with ConnectHER at BCIT

hook.content:
Apprenticeship can take over 6,000 hours. That's years of work, spread across job sites, classrooms, and bureaucratic systems—often without a clear sense of whether you're on track.

Early in our research, we kept hearing the same thing: confusion was pushing people out. Not inability. Not lack of motivation. Just confusion about where they stood and what came next.

We wanted to build something that answered one question clearly: Where am I right now?

problem_framing.content:
The apprenticeship system fragments critical information across three platforms. Hours, competencies, certifications—all siloed.

For neurodivergent apprentices navigating executive function challenges, information overload, and time blindness, fragmentation becomes a genuine barrier to completion.

methods: Persona development based on lived experience
key_findings: Hours, competencies, and certifications live on three separate platforms that don't talk to each other — apprentices were manually cross-referencing systems just to know where they stood
The official SkilledTradesBC portal is desktop-only, which means it's useless on job sites where the actual work happens
Hours discrepancies aren't just administrative errors — apprentices described the uncertainty as a source of real dread, especially before employer check-ins
For neurodivergent apprentices, inconsistent UI patterns and unclear next steps aren't annoying — they're a cognitive tax paid on every login

early_challenges.content:
Scope creep: financial stress, employer communication, exam prep, progress tracking all at once. Paralysis.

Narrowing to neurodivergent apprentices unlocked depth. One audience, one need. Everything else became clear.

pivots.reason: Depth over breadth. Designing for one audience meant decisions became clear instead of compromised.

removedFeatures: Financial planning tools and budget calculators

weightLifted.title: From 6 problem spaces to 1 clear focus
weightLifted.caption: Every feature we removed made space for the ones that actually mattered. Depth over breadth turned a generic tool into something personal.

clarity_scene.title: From 6 problem spaces to 1 clear focus

p0.label: Overloaded
p0.text: Six problem spaces. No clear direction.

p1.label: Subtraction
p1.text: Cut every feature that diluted the product into something generic.

p2.label: Compression
p2.text: One audience. One need. Everything else fell away.

p3.label: Focus
p3.text: Depth over breadth turned a generic tool into something personal.

clarity_scene.caption: Every feature removed made space for what mattered.
clarity_scene.highlight_caption: Every [[feature removed]] made space for the ones that [[mattered]]. [[Depth over breadth]] turned generic into [[personal]].

lofi_phase.title: Wireframes
lofi_phase.content:
Early sketches focused on clarity, not aesthetics. What information matters most? How quickly can someone find it?

Dashboard emerged as central: progress bars, hour counts, next-step prompts. First three seconds must answer 'Where am I?'

prototype.description: Early lo-fidelity wireframes exploring layout, information hierarchy, and the core dashboard interaction.

midfi_embed.title: Mid-Fidelity Wireframes
midfi_embed.caption: Refined mid-fidelity screens showing improved structure and interaction patterns before the final visual pass

hifi_phase.content: Design prioritizes calm: soft colors, generous spacing, clear typography. Dashboard becomes the entry point. Hours completed, hours remaining, competencies achieved—all visible at a glance, not buried in menus.

images.caption: The dashboard provides immediate progress visibility

prototype.description: Browse the complete high-fidelity wireframes — every screen, component, and state in the final design.

development.content: Every feature evaluated against one question: can this be built responsibly? Data syncing and AI features scoped carefully against realistic constraints.
technical_decisions: Chose React Native for cross-platform efficiency
Designed offline-first architecture for job site use
Scoped AI features to avoid overpromising
constraints: No access to official SkilledTradesBC APIs
Team learning curve with mobile development

feature_breakdown.intro: Four core features, each targeting a specific friction point.

features.title: Progress Dashboard
features.description: A centralized view of hours completed, hours remaining, competencies achieved, and certification progress. No digging required—the answer to 'where am I?' is always visible.
features.why: Provides constant orientation. A GPS for the apprenticeship journey.

features.title: Hours Discrepancy Detector
features.description: Compares user-logged hours against official records and flags mismatches. Users can document discrepancies and generate reports for their employers.
features.why: Hours discrepancies create real anxiety. Tools to document and advocate for oneself.

features.title: Financial Resources Hub
features.description: Links to grants, funding programs, and itemized cost breakdowns for apprenticeship expenses.
features.why: Financial information centralized, not scattered across platforms.

features.title: AI Practice Quizzes
features.description: Generates unique practice questions based on trade competencies. Designed to build confidence before official exams.

prototype.description: Walk through each feature. Start with the dashboard, then explore the hours tracker and quiz flow.

validation.content: The design centers on one principle: clarity removes friction. Progress tracking becomes the entry point. Hours discrepancies are surfaced, not hidden.

what_i_learned.reflection: Information alone doesn't create relief. Visibility—removing the need to search—is what produces confidence.

what_i_learned.insight: Scope control is a design skill
what_i_learned.reflection: Saying no to good ideas was harder than coming up with them. We had six problem spaces and had to choose one. That choice — not the features we built — was the most important design decision we made.
Showing incomplete hours and remaining work creates urgency and clarity. The dashboard is the entry point, not a secondary screen.

next_steps.title: Real-Time Data Sync
next_steps.outcome: Hours and milestones update automatically through SkilledTradesBC integration.
next_steps.impact: Removes manual entry — apprentices stay focused on the work, not the paperwork.

next_steps.title: Deeper Accessibility
next_steps.outcome: Screen reader optimization and high-contrast modes reach more learners.
next_steps.impact: The tool becomes genuinely inclusive, not just compliant.

next_steps.title: Offline-Ready Experience
next_steps.outcome: Core features work on job sites with limited or no connectivity.
next_steps.impact: Progress tracking happens where the work happens — not just where the Wi-Fi is.

final_experience.intro:
The prototype below represents the complete flow we envisioned—from opening the app to checking progress, logging hours, and preparing for exams.

As you explore, notice how each screen tries to answer a question before you have to ask it.

prototype.description: Click through the full experience. Start from the dashboard and follow the natural flow.

case_studies.title: InkLink
case_studies.subtitle: Breaking writer's block through collaboration
case_studies.summary: A chain-writing platform that reduces writer's block by turning writing into a shared, low-pressure activity where users contribute one passage at a time.
responsibilities: Led user research and concept validation

hook.content:
Everyone has a story they never finished.

Maybe it's a novel that stalled at chapter three. Maybe it's a screenplay that never made it past the outline. The ideas were there—the momentum wasn't.

InkLink started with a simple question: what if writing didn't have to be a solo activity? What if the pressure of finishing was replaced by the fun of contributing?

problem_framing.content:
Writer's block isn't about running out of ideas. It's about pressure. The blank page. Perfectionism. The weight of solo responsibility.

Sharing that weight removes the block.

methods: Peer surveys with amateur and hobbyist writers
Concept validation sessions to gauge interest
key_findings: Large screens required, not preference. Reading on mobile felt claustrophobic.
Dense layouts killed contribution momentum. People scrolled without stopping to write.
One worked example unlocked confidence. Demonstration, not explanation.

early_challenges.content:
First instinct: build a better editor. Rich formatting, version history, real-time sync.

Then: 'Why not just email a Google Doc?'

Writers didn't need a tool—they needed permission to share authorship. That insight rewired everything.

pivots.reason: Writers consistently preferred larger screens. Mobile felt cramped for both reading and contributing.
Clear single-column reading lets story carry attention, not interface.

pivots.from: Feature-heavy homepage with multiple entry points
pivots.reason: One clear action reduced hesitation. Six options led to choosing none.

removedFeatures: Multiple homepage entry points and discovery filters

weightLifted.title: From a feature-heavy homepage to a single clear action
weightLifted.caption: The homepage had six ways to start. Users chose none. One clear call-to-action made them choose every time.

clarity_scene.title: From a feature-heavy homepage to a single clear action

p1.label: Subtraction
p1.text: Removed competing actions so one choice could lead.

p2.label: Compression
p2.text: Layout collapsed into single path—less scanning, more momentum.

clarity_scene.caption: One CTA beat six options.
clarity_scene.highlight_caption: Homepage had [[six ways to start]]. Users chose [[none]]. One [[clear call-to-action]] made them choose [[every time]].

lofi_phase.content:
Lo-fi work focused on contribution: how to show story history, handle turn-taking, and reward brevity.

Simple pattern emerged: read what came before, add your piece, pass it forward.

images.caption: Early exploration of the contribution flow

hifi_phase.content: High-fidelity designs leaned into typography and whitespace. Color used sparingly to indicate action and turn status. Writing itself stayed neutral, letting each voice come through.

images.caption: Clean reading experience with minimal UI
Writing interface with previous context visible

prototype.description: Experience the reading flow. Tap a story to open it, then scroll through contributions.

development.content:
This project was design-focused, but we kept implementation in mind throughout. Real-time collaboration features were scoped out early—they would have required backend complexity beyond our timeline.

Instead, we designed for asynchronous contribution. Turn-based writing actually fit the concept better: it gave each contributor time to think.
technical_decisions: Designed for asynchronous rather than real-time collaboration
Scoped out complex features like branching narratives
constraints: Real-time sync would require significant backend work
Needed to support multiple device sizes

features.title: Public Story Chains
features.description: Open stories that anyone can contribute to. Writers join, add a passage, and pass the story forward.
features.why: Low barrier to entry. No commitment—just jump in.

features.title: Private Writing Circles
features.description: Invite-only spaces for friends or writing groups. Turn order is set by the creator.

features.title: Timed Contributions
features.description: Optional time limits encourage momentum. If a turn expires, the next writer can step in.
features.why: Deadlines reduce overthinking. A 24-hour window is long enough to be fair, short enough to keep stories moving.

prototype.description: Start a new story, invite a friend, or join an existing chain. Follow the prompts to see each flow.

validation.content: The design removes individual ownership. When writing is shared across multiple contributors, perfectionism loses its grip. Contributions become spontaneous, not labored.

what_i_learned.reflection: Limiting options — one clear CTA, timed turns, single-column reading — reduced friction instead of adding it. Each constraint removed a decision writers would have otherwise agonized over.
Striking layouts create friction. A clear single-column reading experience lets the story carry attention, not the interface.
The homepage had six ways to start. Users chose none. We cut it to one and they started every time. That's the only A/B test we needed.

next_steps.title: Saved Drafts
next_steps.outcome: Writers can step away mid-thought and return without losing momentum.
next_steps.impact: Respects the creative process — not every contribution happens in one sitting.

next_steps.title: Keyboard-First Navigation
next_steps.outcome: Full keyboard and screen reader support across the writing flow.
next_steps.impact: The collaborative experience opens up to every kind of writer.

next_steps.title: Guided First Contribution
next_steps.outcome: New users understand chain writing through doing, not reading.
next_steps.impact: Lowers the barrier from curious visitor to active contributor.

final_experience.intro:
The prototype below shows the complete InkLink experience—from discovering a story to making your first contribution.

Pay attention to how little stands between you and writing. That was intentional.

prototype.description: Click through the full flow. Find a story, read what others have written, and add your own passage.

case_studies.title: SideQuest
case_studies.subtitle: Turning boredom into micro-adventures
case_studies.summary: A web app that helps users discover small, low-commitment activities to break routine, reframing free time as an opportunity rather than a void to fill.

hook.content:
You have a free afternoon. Nothing planned. No obligations.

So you open your phone, scroll through options, and thirty minutes later you're still on the couch—paralyzed by choice, defaulting to nothing.

SideQuest was designed for that moment. Not to give you more options, but to give you one good one.

problem_framing.content:
Boredom isn't about having nothing to do. It's about choosing what to do.

Endless options create endless paralysis.

Flip the model: one suggested thing beats everything you could do.

methods: Persona creation based on peer interviews
Competitive analysis of activity discovery apps
key_findings: Users seek low-pressure activities that require zero planning.
Choice overload causes paralysis and inaction.

embed.title: App Flows
embed.caption: User flow diagrams mapping the core quest discovery journey from idle browsing to quest completion

early_challenges.content:
The first version was a discovery app: filters for location, duration, cost, category. Search bar. 'For You' feed. It was Yelp—but friendlier, with the same problem.

Too many options to choose from prevented action. The concept needed to flip: suggest one thing instead of everything.

pivots.reason: Removing choice removes friction. One button, one option, one moment of decision.
Framing changes how users relate to an activity before they commit.
Warm design signals companionship, not logistics solving.

weightLifted.title: From 5 filters to 1 button
weightLifted.caption: Every filter was another reason not to act. One button removed every excuse.

clarity_scene.title: From 5 filters to 1 button

p1.label: Subtraction
p1.text: Every filter was another reason not to act.

clarity_scene.caption: Every filter was another reason not to act. One button removed every excuse.
clarity_scene.highlight_caption: Every [[filter]] was another reason [[not to act]]. One [[button]] removed [[every excuse]].

lofi_phase.content:
Early wireframes had too many buttons. Filters for location, duration, cost, category—all counterproductive. Users hovered without committing.

Stripped back to one action: 'Give me a quest.' Everything else became optional.

images.caption: Initial designs with too many options

prototype.description: Explore the low-fidelity wireframes. Notice how many options cluttered the screen before we stripped back to a single action.

hifi_phase.content: High-fidelity designs introduced a mascot that guides without feeling childish. Playful language ('quest' not 'activity') and warm colors reduce emotional resistance.

images.caption: The mascot welcomes users with a single clear action
Quest suggestions are simple and actionable
Just enough information to get started

prototype.description: Browse the complete high-fidelity mockups — every screen, component, and interaction state.

development.content: Being researcher, designer, and developer meant feedback loops were tight. Testing findings changed design that afternoon. Code constraints removed features the next morning.
technical_decisions: Built with React for component reusability
Kept quest data local to avoid backend complexity
Prioritized fast load times and immediate interaction
constraints: Solo development meant limited feature scope
No backend meant static quest database
Needed to work well on both mobile and desktop

features.title: Quest Suggestions
features.description: Small, low-commitment activities framed as optional adventures. Each quest includes a title, brief description, and estimated time.
features.why: Low-commitment framing. Just a quest, not an obligation.

features.title: Friendly Guide
features.description: A small mascot character that appears throughout the experience, offering encouragement without being intrusive.
features.why: Warmth without intrusion. Personality without noise.

features.title: Quick Start Flow
features.description: Users can get a quest in two taps. No account required, no preferences to set up first.
features.why: Minimized steps between boredom and action means fewer opportunities to abandon.

prototype.description: Start from the home screen. Get a quest, save it for later, or ask for something different.

validation.content: The mechanic is simple: one button, one suggestion, one moment of decision. Framing matters—'quest' creates expectation of adventure, not obligation.

what_i_learned.reflection: One action is worth more than ten options. First quest hardest. After that, return is automatic.
Every element builds or destroys confidence. Celebration is secondary—the act of choosing is the momentum.

next_steps.title: Personalized Quest Feed
next_steps.outcome: Suggestions evolve based on what you've enjoyed and completed before.
next_steps.impact: Each session feels more like yours — less random, more resonant.

next_steps.title: Location-Aware Suggestions
next_steps.outcome: Quests surface based on what's actually around you right now.
next_steps.impact: Bridges the gap between digital prompt and real-world action.

next_steps.title: Shared Quests
next_steps.outcome: Friends can take on the same quest and compare experiences.
next_steps.impact: Solo adventures gain a social layer without losing their spontaneity.

final_experience.intro:
The presentation below walks through the full SideQuest project—from research and early wireframes to the final high-fidelity solution.

It covers the key design decisions and what was learned.

prototype.description: Full project presentation covering research, process, and final solution.
